result:
The silence in the room is not empty. It’s the thick, velvety kind that arrives after a long argument, or just before a confession. Outside the window, the afternoon light is doing that remarkable thing where it turns ordinary dust into galaxies, swirling in the diagonal beam that cuts from the sash to the worn Persian rug. It’s a light that feels both ancient and urgent, a final, golden offer before the day gives in.

I think about the word "liminal." It’s a word I cling to. It describes thresholds, in-between spaces: doorways, train platforms, the moment between wakefulness and sleep. But sitting here, I realize the grandest liminal space is *now*. This very second. It is always the infinitesimal slice between what just happened and what is about to be. We are never anywhere else. We are perpetual travelers in the corridor, never quite reaching the room we think we’re headed toward, because upon arrival, it instantly becomes another corridor.

The coffee in my cup is no longer hot. It has passed through the prime of its drinkable heat and has entered the tepid acceptance stage. I drink it anyway. There’s a lesson there, too: we spend so much energy trying to hold things at their perfect peak—the perfect temperature, the perfect age, the perfect moment of understanding—that we miss the subtle, complex beauty of the gentle decline. The cold coffee tastes more bitter, yes, but also more true. It has nothing left to hide.

My mind drifts to a conversation I had years ago with my grandfather. He was a man of few words, most of them reserved for the weather or the stubbornness of his old lawnmower. But once, while we were shelling peas on the back porch, he said, "You know, a life is just a collection of small attendings." I must have looked confused. He didn’t elaborate, just nodded toward the bowl of emerald spheres. "Attending to this pea. To the sound of that cardinal. To the ache in your back from sitting too long. The rest is just noise."

I think I’m only now beginning to understand him. We chase the epic narrative, the grand passion, the life-altering revelation. We scroll through curated highlights of other people’s corridors, mistaking them for destinations. Meanwhile, the actual texture of a life is woven from a million tiny, unphotographed threads: the cool side of the pillow, the specific weight of a well-balanced book, the way steam rises from a soup bowl in a predictable yet hypnotic dance, the shared glance with a stranger when a dog does something absurd in the park. These are not the filler between the big events. They are the event. This is what we are attending.

And what of our attention? It has become a fractured currency, the most valuable thing we own and yet we give it away in dribs and drabs to digital ghosts. We pay attention to opinions about things we’ve never experienced, to anger that isn’t ours, to vast abstractions, while the cat’s precise method of curling into a circle on the sun-warmed floor goes unnoticed. To attend—truly attend—to one single thing is a radical act of rebellion in a world screaming for your glance.

The light is fading now. The galactic dust has settled, and the beam has retreated up the wall, a tide of shadow coming in behind it. A chill arrives, the evening’s first. I feel a familiar, sweet melancholy—not sadness, but the keen awareness of a day’s passing. It’s the feeling of being alive in time, which is the only place we can be alive.

I think about love in this fading light. Not the cinematic, sweeping score kind, but the quiet, attending kind. Love as noticing. Love as remembering how they take their tea, or the particular sound of their footsteps on the gravel. Love as the willingness to be bored together, to share silence without the need to fracture it. It is less about grand declarations and more about the ongoing, gentle act of witnessing another human being exist, in all their ordinary, glorious particularity.

We are so afraid of our smallness. We build monuments, seek fame, scramble for legacies, terrified that our single, brief corridor will have meant nothing. But what if meaning isn’t found in the echo we leave in the halls of history? What if it’s found in the quality of our attention within our own, fleeting corridor? The kindness offered for no reason. The creation of something—a meal, a garden, a sentence—for the sheer, private joy of it. The suffering endured with as much grace as we can muster. The courage to feel the devastating beauty of a sunset without immediately reaching for a camera, but simply letting it wash over you, knowing you are the only witness this particular sunset will ever have in exactly this way.

The room is almost dark. I haven’t moved. I haven’t turned on a lamp. I’m attending to the blue hour, that brief, magical threshold between day and night where colors lose their names and the world becomes a silhouette of itself.

Maybe that’s all we are, too: beautiful silhouettes moving through our liminal lives, defined not by our solid mass but by the edges where we meet the light and the dark. Our meaning is not inside us, like a kernel to be unearthed, but in the space we create between each other, in the attention we pay, in the love that is simply a form of focused, grateful noticing.

I get up. My joints creak—a small, private sound in the dark room. I will go and make dinner. I will attend to the sizzle of the onion in the pan, to the rhythmic chop of the knife on the board. I will call a friend just to hear their voice. I will feel the day’s final transition into night.

It is enough. More than enough. This quiet, attended-to life in the endless, beautiful corridor.
